title: SonicWall NGFW IPS and Gateway Anti-Virus Alerts by Signature
dataSources: SonicWall firewall syslog (CIM Network Traffic / IDS), `sourcetype=dell:sonicwall` or `sourcetype=dell:sonicwall:firewall`
spl:
index=netfw (sourcetype="dell:sonicwall" OR sourcetype="dell:sonicwall:firewall") earliest=-24h
| eval sig=coalesce(signature, msg, cat, threat_name)
| eval src_ip=coalesce(src_ip, src, c_src)
| eval dest_ip=coalesce(dest_ip, dst, c_dst)
| where match(lower(sig),"(?i)intrusion|ips|gateway anti|virus|sid") OR match(_raw,"(?i)priority.*(high|critical)")
| stats count by src_ip, dest_ip, sig
| where count>=5
| sort -count
| head 40
description: SonicWall deep packet inspection generates high-fidelity IPS and gateway anti-malware events. Clustering by signature and endpoint pair reveals active exploitation attempts, worm propagation, or policy gaps where expected traffic is misclassified.
value: Security analysts cluster SonicWall IPS and Gateway Anti-Virus alerts by signature to identify automated attacks, worm propagation, and active exploitation attempts, prioritizing response based on CVE severity and MITRE ATT&CK technique mappings.
implementation: Forward SonicWall syslog to Splunk via SC4S or a forwarder; set `linebreaking` per vendor guidance. Install Splunkbase app 6203 for field extractions. Validate whether your deployment uses `dell:sonicwall` or `dell:sonicwall:firewall`. Create lookups for pen-test windows and vulnerability-scanner subnets.
visualization: Table (signature, sources, destinations), Heatmap (time × signature), MITRE tagging overlay in ES if available.
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication
  by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src span=1h
| sort - count

references.title: Splunk Connect for Syslog — Dell SonicWall
Splunkbase — Dell SonicWall Add-on for Splunk

knownFalsePositives: Anomalies from new SaaS app rollouts, browser updates, or vendor URL category changes.
grandmaExplanation: Our SonicWall firewall detects and blocks attacks based on known patterns. We group these detections by pattern type so we can see which attacks are most common and which are most dangerous, helping us decide where to focus our defenses.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• SonicWall NGFW syslog forwarding to a Splunk heavy forwarder. Data in `index=firewall` (or `index=nac` if grouped) with `sourcetype=syslog` or `sourcetype=sonicwall`. Key fields: `msg_id` (IPS event IDs like 82, 83), `pri`, `fw_action`, `Category`, `sid` (signature ID), `src`, `srcPort`, `dst`, `dstPort`, `proto`, `gw_av_sig` (AV signature name), `gw_av_action` (prevented/detected).
• SonicWall syslog uses a key=value format. IPS events have `m=82` (IPS Prevention Alert) or `m=83` (IPS Detection Alert). Gateway Anti-Virus uses `m=24` (GAV). Enable syslog on the SonicWall: Log > Syslog > Add Server.
• Create a `sonicwall_sig_severity.csv` lookup: `sig_id,sig_name,severity,cve,mitre_technique` mapping SonicWall signature IDs to context.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Enable SonicWall syslog: Manage > Log Settings > Syslog > add the HF IP, port 514, facility Local0. Enable IPS, GAV, and Anti-Spyware log categories.

Verify:
```spl
index=firewall sourcetype=sonicwall earliest=-1h
| where msg_id IN ("82","83","24")
| stats count by msg_id
```
msg_id=82: IPS Prevention, msg_id=83: IPS Detection, msg_id=24: GAV.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

Primary search — IPS/GAV signature clustering:
```spl
index=firewall sourcetype=sonicwall earliest=-24h
| where msg_id IN ("82","83","24")
| eval alert_type=case(msg_id="82", "IPS-Prevent", msg_id="83", "IPS-Detect", msg_id="24", "GAV")
| stats count dc(src) as unique_sources dc(dst) as unique_targets values(Category) as categories by sid, alert_type
| lookup sonicwall_sig_severity.csv sig_id as sid OUTPUT sig_name severity cve mitre_technique
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL: Groups IPS and GAV alerts by signature, showing which signatures fire most often, against how many unique sources and targets, and what category they belong to. A single signature hitting many targets suggests a worm or automated scanner. A single signature from many sources suggests a widespread internet attack against a known vulnerability. The CVE and MITRE mappings from the lookup help prioritize response.

Critical signature alert:
```spl
index=firewall sourcetype=sonicwall earliest=-1h
| where msg_id="82" AND fw_action="prevented"
| lookup sonicwall_sig_severity.csv sig_id as sid OUTPUT severity
| where severity="Critical"
| stats count by sid, src, dst, dstPort
```

Step 3 — Validate
(a) In SonicWall: Log > View > filter by IPS. Compare signature IDs with Splunk.
(b) Run an Nmap scan from a test host and verify IPS alerts appear.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard: Row 1 — Top 10 signatures by hit count. Row 2 — IPS Prevent vs Detect ratio. Row 3 — GAV detections with malware family.
Alert: Immediate on Critical severity IPS prevention events.

Runbook:
1. **Critical IPS prevent** — Verify the blocked traffic was malicious. Check CVE for affected software.
2. **IPS Detect (not prevent)** — Signature in detection-only mode. Consider switching to prevention.
3. **GAV high volume from one source** — Internal host may be infected. Isolate and scan.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **No IPS events** — IPS may not be licensed or enabled. Check SonicWall Security Services > Intrusion Prevention.
• **All events are 'detected' not 'prevented'** — IPS may be in detection-only mode. Switch to prevention for critical signatures.
